name: Visual analog pain scale
description: Unidimensional self-reported scale on which the patient marks pain intensity on a continuous 100 mm line

en.name: Visual analog pain scale
en.shortDescription: Unidimensional self-reported scale on which the patient marks pain intensity on a continuous 100 mm line
en.longDescription: ## Definition & Clinical Context The Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for pain is a unidimensional self-reported scale on which the patient marks the intensity of their pain on a continuous line, conventionally 100 mm long, anchored at one end by "no pain" and at the other by "worst pain imaginable" or equivalent wording [1]. The score corresponds to the distance in millimetres from the "no pain" anchor to the patient's mark, yielding a continuous value between 0 and 100. The VAS was popularised for the clinical assessment of pain by Huskisson, who established its sensitivity to change and its applicability across a range of clinical contexts [1]. ## Included Concepts This concept set contains the OMOP-standard SNOMED concept for the scale: - `4165600` — Visual analog pain scale (`273904000`, Staging / Scales, Standard) The non-standard SNOMED Observable Entity `40481449` (Visual analog scale pain score, `443394008`) maps to this standard concept (OMOP `Maps to`), so recorded score values stored against the source code are captured through standard-concept resolution. ## Excluded Concepts The following SNOMED concept is intentionally **not** included: - `40492247` — Assessment using visual analog pain scale (`445719003`, Procedure) — represents the act of assessment, not the recorded score value ## References [1] Huskisson EC. Measurement of pain. *Lancet*. 1974;2(7889):1127-1131. doi:10.1016/s0140-6736(74)90884-8. PMID: 4139420

fr.name: Visual analog pain scale
fr.shortDescription: Échelle unidimensionnelle auto-rapportée sur laquelle le patient marque l'intensité de sa douleur sur une ligne continue de 100 mm
fr.longDescription: ## Définition et contexte clinique La VAS (Visual Analogue Scale, échelle visuelle analogique) pour la douleur est une échelle unidimensionnelle d'auto-évaluation sur laquelle le patient marque l'intensité de sa douleur sur une ligne continue, conventionnellement de 100 mm, ancrée à une extrémité par « aucune douleur » et à l'autre par « la pire douleur imaginable » ou un libellé équivalent [1]. Le score correspond à la distance en millimètres entre l'ancrage « aucune douleur » et la marque du patient, produisant une valeur continue entre 0 et 100. La VAS a été popularisée pour l'évaluation clinique de la douleur par Huskisson, qui en a établi la sensibilité au changement et l'applicabilité dans divers contextes cliniques [1]. ## Concepts inclus Ce concept set contient le concept SNOMED standard OMOP de l'échelle : - `4165600` — Visual analog pain scale (`273904000`, Staging / Scales, Standard) Le concept SNOMED Observable Entity non-standard `40481449` (Visual analog scale pain score, `443394008`) se mappe vers ce concept standard (`Maps to` OMOP) ; les valeurs de score enregistrées sous le code source sont donc capturées via la résolution vers le concept standard. ## Concepts exclus Le concept SNOMED suivant est intentionnellement **non inclus** : - `40492247` — Assessment using visual analog pain scale (`445719003`, Procedure) — représente l'acte d'évaluation, non la valeur du score enregistré ## Références [1] Huskisson EC. Measurement of pain. *Lancet*. 1974;2(7889):1127-1131. doi:10.1016/s0140-6736(74)90884-8. PMID: 4139420

createdByDetails.profession: Medical Informatics and Intensive Care Physician

versions.author: Boris Delange
versions.message: Removed SNOMED Staging/Scales concept 4165600 (Visual analog pain scale, Standard) and SNOMED Procedure concept 40492247 (Assessment using visual analog pain scale). Rationale: this concept set is intended to identify recorded VAS pain score values; only the Observable Entity (40481449, Visual analog scale pain score) is relevant. The Staging/Scales concept represents the tool and the Procedure concept represents the act of assessment, neither carries the score value.

versions.author: Boris Delange
versions.message: Switched to the standard SNOMED concept for the scale. A standard concept (the scale itself) is preferred over a more specific but non-standard 'score' concept, which should not be used directly in OMOP. Reverses the v1.1.1 removal of the Staging/Scales concept.
